summary: Joel shares an update about inspecting Fog Creek Software's new office space, a sunny loft in Manhattan's garment district. He describes his vision for the space including private offices, communal areas, and entertainment amenities. The post focuses primarily on his infrastructure demands to the architect, particularly his insistence on abundant power outlets and flexible cabling. It reflects his broader philosophy that developer workspace quality directly impacts productivity.
key_points: Private windowed offices are treated as a non-negotiable requirement for the new Fog Creek office
Joel demands dramatically more power infrastructure than typical offices, specifying 4 outlets per foot of wall space
The office design prioritizes both individual workspace quality (private offices) and communal spaces (living room, kitchenette, pool table)
Flexible network infrastructure is a priority, with the ability to run LAN, telephone, fiber, and cable TV wiring even if exposed
money_quotes: We need three times as many power outlets as anyone would think.
I have ten things plugged in right at my desk.
I specified 4 outlets every foot, is that absurd?
key_insight: A great developer workspace requires treating infrastructure like power and networking as first-class concerns, not afterthoughts.
